en.extract:
The rusty-backed monjita (Neoxolmis rubetra) is a species of bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is endemic to Argentina but has been recorded as a vagrant in Brazil and Chile. 


Taxonomy
The rusty-backed monjita has a complicated taxonomic history. It was formally described in 1860 as "Taenioptera Rubetra". In 1927 it was moved to genus Xolmis. Late in the twentieth century some authors moved it to genus Neoxolmis that had been erected in 1927 for what became the chocolate-vented tyrant (N. rufiventris); others retained it in Xolmis. A 2018 study determined it did not belong in Xolmis and two 2020 studies moved it to genus Nengetus. The South American Classification Committee of the American Ornithological Society (SACC) restored it instead to genus Neoxolmis. The IOC and the Clements taxonomy soon adopted that change. However, as of December 2024 BirdLife International's Handbook of the Birds of the World retained the species in Xolmis. Because the reassignment to Neoxolmis resulted in species of several genera having the English name "monjita", the SACC is seeking a proposal to consider English name changes.
The rusty-backed monjita is monotypic. However, what is now the salinas monjita (N. salinarum) was originally described as a subspecies of the rusty-backed.


Description
The rusty-backed monjita is 18 to 19 cm (7.1 to 7.5 in) long. Adult males have a rufescent crown and a long white supercilium on an otherwise black-streaked white face. Their upperparts are mostly rufescent or rufous-brown with a grayish white rump. Their wings are mostly black. Their wing's greater and median coverts have grayish white edges and tips, the lesser coverts are rufescent, and the tertials have white edges. Their tail is mostly black with white outer webs on the three outer pairs of feathers. Their throat and underparts are mostly white with black streaks on the sides of the neck and across the breast and a rufous tinge on the flanks. Adult females are duller and less rufescent than males. Both sexes have a dark iris, a black bill, and black legs and feet. Immatures have dull buffy upperparts and a buffish throat and breast.


Distribution and habitat
The rusty-backed monjita is found in Argentina in an area roughly bounded by the provinces of Tucumán, Entre Ríos, and northeastern  Santa Cruz. In addition, it has been recorded as a vagrant in extreme southern Brazil and in Chile. The species inhabits grasslands and steppe with scattered small bushes; it sometimes is found along the shores of lakes. In elevation it ranges up to 1,000 m (3,300 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The rusty-backed monjita is a complete migrant. It breeds in an area roughly bounded by the Argentinian provinces of Mendoza, southern Buenos Aires, and Santa Cruz. It entirely vacates that area for the austral winter, moving north and east from the line Mendoza-Buenos Aires as far as 
Tucumán and Entre Ríos.


Feeding
The rusty-backed monjita feeds on insects. During the breeding season it primarily forages in pairs or groups of up to about five; in winter it joins in flocks as large as 30 individuals.  It is often terrestrial, walking or running with pauses to scan or capture prey, but it will perch on bushes, wires, or fence posts and drop onto prey.


Breeding
The rusty-backed monjita breeds between September and March. Males make a display flight during which their wings make a metallic rattle. The species' nest is an open cup made from twigs and grass lined with feathers. It is placed on the ground, usually under a small bush. One known nest had two chicks. The typical clutch size, incubation period, time to fledging, and details of parental care are not known.


Vocalization
As of May 2025 xeno-canto had a single recording of a rusty-backed monjita vocalization; the Cornell Lab of Ornithology's Macaulay Library had it and one other. The species' song has not been described.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the rusty-backed monjita as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered uncommon to locally fairly common. "Habitat has been severely altered by overgrazing and deforestation, and native grassland and Prosopis forests virtually eliminated over large areas; as a result, the species has declined."


References

The '''rusty-backed monjita''' ('''''Neoxolmis rubetra''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers.<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=3 March 2025 }}</ref> It is [[endemic]] to [[Argentina]] but has been recorded as a [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] in [[Brazil]] and [[Chile]].<ref name=SACCcountries>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 March 2025. Species Lists of Birds for South American Countries and Territories. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCCountryLists.htm retrieved 30 March 2025</ref> 

==Taxonomy==

The rusty-backed monjita has a complicated taxonomic history. It was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1860 as "''Taenioptera Rubetra''".<ref>{{cite journal | last=Burmeister |first=H. |title=Systematiches Verzeichniss der in den La Plata-Staaten beobachteten Vögelarten | date= 1853 | journal=Journal für Ornithologie | publisher=Friedländer |number=46 |page=247 | language=German |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14327887 |access-date=May 13, 2025 }}</ref> In 1927 it was moved to genus ''[[Xolmis]]''.<ref>Cory, C. B., and C. E. Hellmayr. 1927. "Catalogue of birds of the Americas". ''Field Museum Nat. History Publ., Zool. Ser.'', vol. 13, pt. 5. pages 14–15.</ref> Late in the twentieth century some authors moved it to genus ''[[Neoxolmis]]'' that had been erected in 1927 for what became the [[chocolate-vented tyrant]] (''N. rufiventris''); others retained it in ''Xolmis''.<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 March 2025. Ser.'', vol. 13, pt. 5. page 39.</ref> A 2018 study determined it did not belong in ''Xolmis'' and two 2020 studies moved it to genus ''Nengetus''.<ref>Fjeldså, J., J. I. Ohlson, H. Batalha-Filho, P. G. P. Ericson, and M. the fluvicoline tyrant flycatchers (Passeriformes, Tyrannidae, Fluvicolinae) | journal=Zootaxa| volume=4747 | issue=1 | pages=167–176 | doi=10.11646/zootaxa.4747.1.7 | doi-access=free | pmid=32230123 | url=https://nrm.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1511256/FULLTEXT01 }}</ref> The South American Classification Committee of the [[American Ornithological Society]] (SACC) restored it instead to genus editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2021 | title=Tyrant flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 11.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/flycatchers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=14 July 2021 }}</ref><ref Available at: https://datazone.birdlife.org/about-our-science/taxonomy retrieved 23 December 2024</ref> Because the reassignment to ''Neoxolmis'' resulted in species of several genera having the English name "monjita", the SACC is seeking a proposal to consider English name changes.<ref name=SACClist/>

The rusty-backed monjita is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC15.1/> However, what is now the [[salinas monjita]] (''N. salinarum'') was originally described as a subspecies of the rusty-backed.<ref>Nores, M. and Yzurieta, D. (1979). "Una nueva especie y dos nuevas subespecies de aves (Passeriformes)". ''Misc. Acad. Nac. Cienc. Córdoba'' 61: 4-8.</ref>

==Description==

The rusty-backed monjita is {{convert|18|to|19|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long. Adult males have a rufescent crown and a long white [[supercilium]] on an otherwise black-streaked white face. Their upperparts are mostly rufescent or rufous-brown with a grayish white rump. Their wings are mostly black. Their wing's greater and median [[coverts]] have grayish white edges and tips, the lesser coverts are rufescent, and the [[tertials]] have white edges. Their tail is mostly black with white outer webs on the three outer pairs of feathers. Their throat and underparts are mostly white with black streaks on the sides of the neck and across the breast and a rufous tinge on the flanks. Adult females are duller and less rufescent than males. Both sexes have a dark iris, a black bill, and black legs and feet. Immatures have dull buffy upperparts and a buffish throat and breast.<ref name=RBMO-BOW>Farnsworth, A. and G. Langham (2021). Rusty-backed Monjita (''Neoxolmis rubetra''), version 1.1. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.rubmon1.01.1 retrieved May 13, 2025</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The rusty-backed monjita is found in Argentina in an area roughly bounded by the provinces of [[Tucumán Province|Tucumán]], [[Entre Ríos Province|Entre Ríos]], and northeastern  [[Santa Cruz Province, Argentina|Santa Cruz]].<ref name=RBMO-BOW/><ref name=Peña>{{cite book | last =de la Peña | first =Martín R. | last2=Rumboll |first2=Maurice|  title =Birds of Southern South America and Antarctica | publisher =Princeton University Press | series =Princeton Illustrated Checklists | date =2001 | location =New Jersey | pages =Plate 75, map 75.7 | isbn =0-691-09035-1 }}</ref> In addition, it has been recorded as a vagrant in extreme southern Brazil and in Chile.<ref name=SACCcountries/> The species inhabits grasslands and [[Temperate grasslands, savannas, and shrublands|steppe]] with scattered small bushes; it sometimes is found along the shores of lakes. In elevation it ranges up to {{convert|1000|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=RBMO-BOW/><ref name=Peña/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The rusty-backed monjita is a complete migrant. It breeds in an area roughly bounded by the Argentinian provinces of [[Mendoza Province|Mendoza]], southern [[Buenos Aires Province|Buenos Aires]], and Santa Cruz. It entirely vacates that area for the [[South|austral]] winter, moving north and east from the line Mendoza-Buenos Aires as far as 
Tucumán and Entre Ríos.<ref name=RBMO-BOW/><ref name=Peña/>

===Feeding===

The rusty-backed monjita feeds on insects. During the breeding season it primarily forages in pairs or groups of up to about five; in winter it joins in flocks as large as 30 individuals.  It is often terrestrial, walking or running with pauses to scan or capture prey, but it will perch on bushes, wires, or fence posts and drop onto prey.<ref name=RBMO-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The rusty-backed monjita breeds between September and March. Males make a display flight during which their wings make a metallic rattle. The species' nest is an open cup made from twigs and grass lined with feathers. It is placed on the ground, usually under a small bush. One known nest had two chicks. The typical clutch size, incubation period, time to fledging, and details of parental care are not known.<ref name=RBMO-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Neoxolmis-rubetra |species=the rusty-backed monjita}}
===Vocalization===

As of May 2025 [[xeno-canto]] had a single recording of a rusty-backed monjita vocalization; the [[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]]'s [[Macaulay Library]] had it and one other.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Neoxolmis-rubetra |title=Rusty-backed Monjita ''Neoxolmis rubetra'' |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=2025 |website=xeno-canto |access-date=May 13, 2025 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://search.macaulaylibrary.org/catalog?taxonCode=rubmon1&mediaType=audio&sort=rating_rank_desc |title=Rusty-backed Monjita ''Neoxolmis rubetra'' |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=2025 |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=May 13, 2025}}</ref> The species' song has not been described.<ref name=RBMO-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the rusty-backed monjita as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered uncommon to locally fairly common. "Habitat has been severely altered by overgrazing and deforestation, and native grassland and ''Prosopis'' forests virtually eliminated over large areas; as a result, the species has declined."<ref name=RBMO-BOW/>
